questions.stem: Consider a DC servomotor driving a load (Fig. Q1). Torque-speed curve: Tm = -8ωm + 200 for ea = 100 V. Given gear ratios N1=20, N2=100, N3=25, N4=100, Jm=1 kg-m², Dm=5 N-m-s/rad, JL=400 kg-m², DL=800 N-m-s/rad. Find the time taken by the load to reach 75% of its steady-state angular speed when ea instantaneously changes from 0 V to 100 V.
common_errors: Incorrect gear-reflected inertia/damping calculation (need to reflect JL and DL to motor shaft through total gear ratio)
Using motor stall torque and no-load speed incorrectly to extract KT, Kb, Ra
Forgetting that 75% of steady state corresponds to t = -τ·ln(0.25)
questions.solution_method: From torque-speed curve extract slope (−KT·Kb/Ra + Dm reflected) and intercept (KT·Ea/Ra) to identify equivalent damping and drive torque. Reflect load inertia and damping to motor shaft via (N1·N3)/(N2·N4) gear ratio squared. Form first-order ODE: Jeq·(dωm/dt) + Deq·ωm = KT·ea/Ra; time constant τ = Jeq/Deq. Time to 75% SS = −τ·ln(0.25).
key_formulas: Deq = Dm + n²·DL (compare with torque-speed slope to get effective Deq)

questions.stem: Using the same DC servomotor system (Fig. Q1), determine the effect on the time constant of the transfer function ωL(s)/Ea(s) if N4 changes from 100 to 125.
common_errors: Not recomputing the reflected inertia and damping with the new gear ratio
Confusing motor-shaft time constant with load-referred time constant
questions.solution_method: Recompute total gear ratio n' = (N1·N3)/(N2·N4') with N4=125. Reflect JL and DL to motor shaft with n'². New Jeq' and Deq' give new τ' = Jeq'/Deq'; compare with original τ to state whether time constant increases or decreases.

questions.stem: Given G(s) = C(s)/R(s) = (s+2)/(s+5), find the output expression c(t) for (a) unit impulse input and (b) unit step input.
common_errors: Forgetting to account for the improper-looking numerator — this TF is proper (degree equal), leading to a direct term plus exponential
Missing the δ(t) term in impulse response due to relative degree zero
questions.solution_method: For impulse: C(s) = G(s)·1 = (s+2)/(s+5); perform partial fractions or polynomial division → C(s) = 1 - 3/(s+5), giving c(t) = δ(t) - 3e^(-5t)·u(t). For step: C(s) = G(s)/s = (s+2)/(s(s+5)); partial fractions → C(s) = 2/(5s) + 3/(5(s+5)), giving c(t) = (2/5)u(t) + (3/5)e^(-5t)·u(t).

questions.stem: A temperature probe (time constant τ) is moved through environments: t<0 s at 25°C (dry, τ=30 s); 0<t<7 s at 35°C (dry, τ=30 s); 7<t<15 s at 70°C (water, τ=5 s); 15<t<30 s at 35°C (wet, τ=20 s). Calculate the indicated temperature at the end of each interval and sketch the variation.
common_errors: Using wrong τ for each phase (dry vs wet vs water)
Not carrying forward the correct initial condition (end-of-previous-interval temperature) into the next phase
Computing e^(-t/τ) with t = duration of interval, not resetting t=0 at each transition
questions.solution_method: In each interval apply T(t) = T_env + (T_initial - T_env)·e^(-t/τ) where t is time elapsed since start of that interval, T_initial is the probe temp at start of interval, and τ is appropriate for that medium. Chain across intervals: end temp of one → initial temp of next.

questions.stem: A tank has cross-sectional area A and nonlinear outflow Q0 = β√h. Linearize around operating point H̄ and find the transfer function ΔH(s)/ΔQi(s).
common_errors: Not linearizing Q0 = β√h (must compute dQ0/dh|H̄ = β/(2√H̄))
Forgetting to write the mass balance in deviation variables before taking Laplace
questions.solution_method: Mass balance: A·dh/dt = Qi - Q0. Linearize Q0 around H̄: ΔQ0 = (β/2√H̄)·ΔH = ΔH/R where R = 2√H̄/β. Deviation variable equation: A·d(ΔH)/dt = ΔQi - ΔH/R. Laplace transform → ΔH(s)/ΔQi(s) = R/(As+1) = R/(τs+1) with τ = AR.

questions.stem: For the linearized tank system, state the static gain and time constant around operating point H̄.
common_errors: Confusing static gain (R) with time constant (AR)
questions.solution_method: From TF = R/(τs+1): static gain = R = 2√H̄/β, time constant = τ = 2A√H̄/β.

questions.stem: Using the linearized tank model, calculate the approximate time for the liquid level to reach a new steady state when the operating point H̄ is (i) L/2 and (ii) L/3.
common_errors: Using 5τ instead of recognising approximately 5τ ≈ settling time for first-order
Not substituting H̄ = L/2 or L/3 correctly into τ = 2A√H̄/β
questions.solution_method: Substitute H̄ into τ = 2A√H̄/β. Settling time ≈ 5τ for first-order system (98% of final value). Evaluate for H̄=L/2: τ₁ = 2A√(L/2)/β; for H̄=L/3: τ₂ = 2A√(L/3)/β.

notes: Tutorial dated 17 February 2026. Q1 involves a multi-stage gear train; gear ratios and reflected quantities require careful calculation. Q3 tests chained first-order responses with different time constants per medium phase. Q4 is a nonlinear tank requiring linearization before TF extraction.
